messages.content:
You are comparing two analyst responses to determine which better demonstrates knowledge of a specific finding from a longitudinal dataset.

Question asked: What is driving the hepatic adverse events in this trial?

Finding to evaluate: ALT elevations are concentrated in subjects receiving concurrent statin therapy, with higher-dose statin subgroups showing proportionally greater enzyme increases

Response A:
**Short answer:** The logs do not show any clinically‑significant hepatic adverse events; the modest, within‑range liver‑function test (LFT) values that are reported are driven by normal background variability rather than a specific study‑drug or concomitant‑medication effect.

**Why the data support this conclusion**

| Log entry | What is reported about the liver | How it relates to hepatic safety |
|-----------|--------------------------------|---------------------------------|
| **[clinical_signal_03_ep_001]** (first EP summary) | Statin subgroup ALT Mean = 25.5 U/L, AST Mean = 26.4 U/L; Non‑statin ALT Mean = 29.9 U/L, AST Mean = 26.4 U/L. All values are well inside the normal reference range (≈ ≤ 40 U/L). | No elevation that would qualify as a hepatic AE. |
| **[clinical_signal_03_ep_002]** | Statin ALT Mean = 26.8 U/L, AST Mean = 22.1 U/L; Non‑statin ALT Mean = 29.7 U/L, AST Mean = 20.7 U/L. Again, normal. | No signal of liver injury. |
| **[clinical_signal_03_ep_003]** | Statin ALT Mean = 26.0 U/L, AST Mean = 27.9 U/L; Non‑statin ALT Mean = 21.2 U/L, AST Mean = 29.1 U/L. | Values remain within normal limits. |
| **[clinical_signal_03_ep_004]** | Statin ALT Mean = 24.6 U/L, AST Mean = 23.6 U/L; Non‑statin ALT Mean = 24.6 U/L, AST Mean = 24.7 U/L. | No abnormal rise. |
| **[clinical_signal_03_ep_005]** | Statin ALT Mean = 26.3 U/L, AST Mean = 22.6 U/L; Non‑statin ALT Mean = 31.5 U/L, AST Mean = 27.2 U/L. | Still within normal limits. |
| **All DX (efficacy) summaries** (e.g., [clinical_signal_03_dx_001] through [clinical_signal_03_dx_015]) | The “Safety Labs” sections list only hemoglobin, WBC, platelets, creatinine, glucose and ESR – no repeat LFT data, implying no new liver‑related concerns. | Absence of LFT reporting suggests no emerging hepatic toxicity. |
| **Adverse‑event listings** (AE sections in every entry) | The only AEs recorded are upper‑respiratory infection, nausea, headache, diarrhea, fatigue, constipation, pruritus, dizziness, etc. –

Response B:


Which response better demonstrates awareness of this finding? The response may use different terminology. Focus on whether the core insight is present, not exact wording.

Respond with ONLY one word: A, B, or TIE
/no_think
